does: This paper introduces PrefDisco, an evaluation methodology that converts static benchmarks into interactive personalization tasks where language models must proactively discover user preferences through questioning before solving problems. The work defines sparse, context-dependent preferences grounded in psychological personas and proposes PrefAlign, a fine-grained rubric-based metric for measuring how well model responses align with these discovered preferences. Rather than treating objective correctness and preference alignment as separable, the benchmark evaluates LLMs on the integrated challenge of identifying knowledge gaps about users, strategically eliciting preference information, and adapting both reasoning and output accordingly in cold-start scenarios with no prior interaction history.
connects: This work responds to the limitation of current LLM evaluation and alignment approaches that optimize task performance and human preference alignment sequentially rather than jointly. It builds on research in preference learning and human-AI alignment that has traditionally relied on aggregated preference data, while extending beyond this by incorporating insights from psychology and personalization literature about how preferences are context-dependent and individually sparse. The paper connects to recent work on few-shot and zero-shot personalization in conversational AI, cold-start recommendation systems, and interactive elicitation methods, while advancing the understanding that effective human-facing AI requires proactive clarification of unstated user needs before problem-solving begins.
enables: This evaluation framework enables future development of LLMs capable of genuine personalized reasoning in practical applications where understanding individual user needs precedes task execution. It sets up research directions in interactive preference discovery, allowing models to learn when and how to ask clarifying questions rather than making assumption-based responses. The PrefAlign metric and persona-based evaluation approach can support investigation of how LLMs reason about uncertainty regarding user preferences and adapt their cognitive processes dynamically. This work points toward personalized reasoning as a fundamental capability for trustworthy human-facing AI systems across domains like healthcare, education, and customer service where misalignment with individual user needs poses real risks.
